title: Busy-ness
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Being Too Busy
summary: Scripture challenges the modern cult of busyness, presenting rest as sacred, presence as more valuable than productivity, and hurry as incompatible with the life God intends.
body:
Busyness has become the default answer to how are you in modern culture. Scripture offers a different assessment. The psalmist hears God say: be still, and know that I am God [verse:ps.46.10]. Stillness is not wasted time — it is the space where the most important knowing happens.

Martha is the Bible's patron saint of busyness. She is distracted by all the preparations, while Mary sits at Jesus' feet. When Martha complains, Jesus does not validate her urgency: Martha, Martha, you are worried and upset about many things, but only one thing is needed [verse:luke.10.41-42]. The good is the enemy of the best.

Ecclesiastes observes that there is a season for everything [verse:eccl.3.1-8]. The problem with chronic busyness is that it collapses all seasons into one: the season of doing. There is no season for reflecting, for resting, for simply being present. The Teacher's verdict on relentless labor is telling: it is meaningless, a chasing after wind [verse:eccl.4.6].

Jesus' life was full but never frantic. He withdrew regularly to pray [verse:mark.1.35]. He slept. He attended weddings and dinner parties. His pace was set by his Father's agenda, not by the crowd's demands.

The Sabbath principle is God's structural answer to busyness [verse:exod.20.8-10]. One day in seven, stop. Not because there is nothing to do, but because your identity is not your output. You are a child of God before you are a worker, and rest is the recurring reminder.

verses.annotation: Be still and know that I am God — stillness as the context for the most important knowledge.
Martha's busyness gently rebuked — presence with Jesus is the one thing needed.
A time for everything — busyness collapses all seasons into one relentless push.
Better one handful with tranquility than two handfuls with toil — less can be more.
Jesus rising early to pray in solitude — prioritizing communion with God over crowd demands.
The Sabbath commandment — a structural guardrail against endless activity.
Come to me, all who are weary — Jesus offers rest, not another task list.

scholars.quote: Goldingay reads Psalm 46:10 as a divine command that cuts against the human instinct to fix everything through action — 'be still' is not passive but a deliberate posture of trust that God is in control.

Garrett interprets Ecclesiastes' teaching on time and seasons as a corrective to the illusion of control — the wise person recognizes that not every moment is a moment for doing; some are for receiving.

Keener observes that Luke's portrait of Jesus consistently shows a man unhurried by urgent need, suggesting that kingdom effectiveness comes from alignment with God's priorities, not from maximum activity.
scholars.source: A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Keener, 1999)
